rationale: This motivation challenge can be addressed by clarifying why NAS is needed beyond standard hyperparameter tuning through empirical ablations, search-space justification, complexity/runtime comparisons, and evidence that simple tuning underperforms on the same benchmarks and constraints.

Providing a clearer theoretical intuition and empirical ablations explaining why and when topological constraints promote factor-wise variation can be added in rebuttal without changing the method, making this concern largely addressable by explanation and analysis.

The reviewer asks for clarification on how specific targets in a table were derived and why they are desirable, which can be explained with methodological details, derivations, or references in a rebuttal without new experiments.

Providing the specific NER model name/citation and adding an accuracy/error analysis of the NER component’s impact on evaluation are details and auxiliary experiments that can be included in a rebuttal or camera-ready without changing the core method or results collection pipeline.

If an experimental parameter r is used but not identified or defined in section 3.2, the authors can clarify and specify it in the rebuttal and camera-ready with a precise definition and sensitivity analysis, making this an easily fixable documentation issue.
Clarity and presentation issues can be directly addressed in a rebuttal by clarifying the method, reorganizing explanations, and promising/including revised text and diagrams.

Seed sensitivity can be empirically addressed by reporting variance across multiple random seeds and, if needed, adding stabilizing details, so authors can answer with additional experiments and clarifications in a rebuttal.

Providing additional experimental results measuring accuracy under controlled input shifts for shift-sensitive baselines is a straightforward analysis that authors can run and include in the rebuttal/supplementary material.

A request to describe supplementary experiments validating the prompt strategy can be addressed in a rebuttal by reporting additional runs, ablations, or references to already-conducted but omitted experiments and summarizing their results.

A broad judgment that the contribution is insufficient empirically or theoretically typically requires adding new experiments, proofs, or major methodological advances beyond the rebuttal’s scope, so it cannot be fully resolved in a short response.

The concern is about interpretability and principled meaning of the weighting parameter, which authors could address in rebuttal by clarifying theory, providing sensitivity analyses, calibration procedures, or empirical mappings from weights to outcome changes, without changing the core method.
Clarifying the definition of “evolutionary PDEs” is a terminology/notation issue that can be addressed by adding an explicit early definition and minor text edits without changing technical content.

The reviewer asks for analyses linking results to training data, which authors could address by reporting known training corpus details, running additional controlled tests across models with different documented datasets, or adding caveats if data are unavailable.

Explaining why certain baselines are omitted or adding their results to figures is a straightforward addition or clarification that can be provided in a rebuttal and, if needed, in a camera-ready revision.

Adding missing citations and running or discussing comparisons to closely related prior Reader-Retriever EL work is typically addressable in a rebuttal via updated related-work discussion and, if needed, quick supplementary experiments or analysis.

The concern about fair backbone parity in comparisons can be addressed in rebuttal by reporting or quickly adding experiments that re-run the method and/or baselines with matched backbones or by providing ablations showing performance under the same backbones.

Reporting variability across multiple random seeds and adding standard deviations is a straightforward addition of results and protocol details that can be provided in a rebuttal and camera-ready without changing the core method.
Adding or updating baseline results (e.g., stronger GNN baselines from BUDDY) is an experimental completeness issue that can be addressed in rebuttal by providing new comparisons or analyses.

Authors can partially address this by clarifying rationale for benchmark choice, adding or citing additional evaluations (if feasible within rebuttal or appendix), and providing analysis of generalization, but fully expanding experiments across many benchmarks may require new runs beyond rebuttal time, so it is only partially fixable via argument and limited add-on results.

The concern asks for clarification of the method’s unique value relative to specific baselines, which can be addressed in a rebuttal by providing comparative analysis, ablation insights, broader metrics, qualitative benefits, and clarifying claims/goals.
Presentation issues like misleading figure coloring and improving experimental clarity are fixable by revising plots and analysis descriptions without changing the core method.

Authors can clarify their recommendation on data sufficiency, justify why more data may or may not help, and add analyses (e.g., learning curves or GEM comparisons) to assess the value of additional data or alternative resources within a rebuttal and minor revisions.

Providing a JAX interface for parallelized execution is an implementation/engineering request that can be answered (feasibility, design) and partially addressed by a prototype or plan within a rebuttal, though full implementation may exceed rebuttal time.

This is a request for a clarification/definition mismatch of symbols (Y vs U) that can be resolved in a rebuttal by explicitly defining Y, relating it to the utility U, and adjusting notation or adding explanatory text.
This is a descriptive observation about the paper’s novelty rather than a critique, so it can be clarified or explained in a rebuttal without requiring new experiments or changes.
Grammar and writing quality issues can be corrected during rebuttal/camera-ready with edits and proofreading tools, without altering the paper’s core technical content.

The authors can detail their hyperparameter search protocol (search space, selection criteria, budgets, seeds) and report whether exhaustive combinations were tried, which is fully answerable in a rebuttal without new experiments.

A novelty critique about the work being mainly engineering is a high-level value judgment that cannot be overturned by a short rebuttal, except by reframing claims or conceding scope rather than adding substantive new ideas.
Terminology ambiguity about the term “passage” can be clarified in a rebuttal by defining it precisely and distinguishing it from retrieved candidates, possibly with revised wording and examples.

A request for more discussion of mechanisms and insights can be addressed by adding analysis, ablations, and explanatory text in the rebuttal and camera-ready without changing the core method or experiments.

The reviewer questions the core motivation versus simple hyperparameter tuning, which can be addressed in rebuttal by clarifying problem setting, search space differences, baselines, empirical comparisons to strong HPO, and providing ablations or additional experiments demonstrating benefits beyond tuning.

Providing a clear, theory- or intuition-based explanation for why topological constraints promote disentanglement can be added in rebuttal via expanded motivation, conceptual arguments, simplified examples, and ablations without changing the core method or results.

Explaining how the targets in a table were derived and why they are desirable can be clarified with methodological details, definitions, and justification in a rebuttal without changing the core method or results.

Providing the specific NER model and its citation, along with reporting its accuracy and an analysis of how NER errors might affect the evaluation, can be added in a rebuttal/supplementary with experiments or ablations using existing logs and reruns.

Clarifying and specifying the r parameter (definition, choice, sensitivity) in the methods section is a straightforward omission that can be addressed in a rebuttal and camera-ready via added description and analyses.
Clarity and presentation issues can typically be addressed in a rebuttal by clarifying the method description, outlining contributions explicitly, and providing schematic summaries or revisions.
Seed sensitivity can be assessed and mitigated by running multiple random seeds and reporting variance or adding stabilizing procedures, which can be done and evidenced within a rebuttal.

Providing additional experimental results quantifying accuracy under input shifts for shift-sensitive models is a straightforward empirical addition that can be produced and summarized in a rebuttal/supplement.

Requests for details on supplementary experiments or additional validations can be addressed in a rebuttal by describing existing runs, ablations, or preliminary follow-ups, and summarizing their results or providing pointers to included/appendix material.

A broad judgment that the paper’s overall empirical or theoretical contribution is insufficient typically requires adding new results or reframing claims, which cannot be fully remedied in a short rebuttal without substantial new experiments or proofs.

The concern asks for a principled or interpretable notion of magnitude (lambda), which would require new theory, metrics, or experiments beyond minor clarifications, so it cannot be fully resolved in a short rebuttal without conceding the limitation or deferring to future work.
Clarifying the definition of “evolutionary PDEs” is a straightforward definitional/terminology issue that can be addressed in a rebuttal and by minor text edits to the paper.

The reviewer asks for analysis linking results to training data, which authors can address by reporting known training data details, ablation/correlation analyses if available, or acknowledging limits and adding preliminary evidence or discussion in rebuttal.
Explaining why some baselines/algorithms are omitted or adding their results is typically feasible during rebuttal by providing clarifications or supplementary experiments/plots.

Adding missing citations and running or reporting a comparison to a specific recent Reader-Retrieval EL work can be addressed in a rebuttal via literature updates and, if needed, quick supplementary experiments or discussion clarifying differences.

This is a fair comparability/ablation request that can be addressed in rebuttal by reporting or committing to run results with matched backbones or providing controlled ablations clarifying backbone effects.

Reporting results over multiple random seeds with means and standard deviations, adding run counts, and detailing experimental settings is straightforward to provide in a rebuttal and supplementary update without altering the core method.

Adding or updating missing baseline comparisons (e.g., improved BUDDY/GNN baselines) is a standard empirical omission that can be remedied in rebuttal with additional experiments, results, and discussion.

Authors can add results on additional public benchmarks or provide cross-dataset generalization tests and ablations within the rebuttal/supplement, directly addressing the breadth of evaluation and generalizability claims.

The reviewer questions the comparative advantage and motivation given similar baselines, which can be addressed by clarifying unique benefits, ablations, broader metrics (e.g., exact equivariance, model-agnosticism, training-free replacement), and additional analyses or experiments in rebuttal/appendix without changing the core method.

Presentation issues in experimental figures (e.g., misleading colors) and calls for improved analysis can be addressed in rebuttal and camera-ready by clarifying plots, re-coloring, adding legends, and expanding analyses without altering the core method.

The concern asks for clarification of the paper’s takeaways about data quantity and possible alternatives (e.g., GEMs), which can be addressed by explicating rationale, adding analysis, or minor experiments or discussion in a rebuttal/appendix without changing the core method.

Providing a JAX interface or clarifying feasibility and planned support is an implementation/interface question that authors can address in rebuttal by outlining design, constraints, and preliminary timelines or providing a prototype, so it is answerable/fixable.

This is a notation/clarity question about what Y represents and its relation to U, which can be directly clarified and aligned in a rebuttal (and with minor text edits) without changing the method or results.
This is a descriptive restatement of the contribution rather than a critique, so there is nothing to rebut beyond clarifying intent and positioning relative to prior work.
Grammar and writing quality issues can be directly corrected during the rebuttal and camera-ready phases using editing and proofreading tools without changing the technical content.

Clarifying the hyperparameter selection process (search space, method, budget, and whether exhaustive combinations were tried) is a procedural detail that authors can fully explain and, if needed, supplement with limited additional experiments during rebuttal.

Whether the work contains sufficient novelty is a matter of research contribution framing and community standards rather than something that can be fixed with new experiments in a short rebuttal, so authors can only argue and clarify positioning but not fundamentally change the paper’s novelty.

Clarifying the terminology and defining “passage” precisely (e.g., whether it denotes retrieved text spans, candidate entities/relations, or input segments) is a straightforward specification issue that can be resolved in a rebuttal by adding clear definitions and consistent usage.

A request to add more discussion and insights about the method’s mechanisms can be addressed in a rebuttal by providing explanations, ablations, and theoretical or intuitive analysis without changing the core results.

This motivation-level concern can be addressed in rebuttal by providing empirical comparisons against strong hyperparameter tuning baselines and clarifying why NAS yields benefits beyond tuning despite limited layers/operations.

Providing an intuitive and theoretical explanation for why topological constraints aid disentanglement can be added in rebuttal via expanded motivation, formal connections, ablations, and illustrative examples without altering the core method or results.

The reviewer asks for clarification on how specific targets were derived and why they are desirable, which can be explained by detailing the derivation procedure, design choices, and intended properties in the rebuttal without changing the core method or results.
Specifying the exact NER model and adding an accuracy/error analysis of its impact on evaluation can be provided in a rebuttal with supplemental experiments and details.

Clarifying and specifying a missing experimental hyperparameter (the r parameter) can be directly addressed in a rebuttal by defining it, explaining its role, and providing sensitivity analyses or updated text for Section 3.2.
Clarity and presentation issues can typically be addressed in a rebuttal by clarifying scope, reorganizing exposition, and specifying contributions more explicitly.

Seed sensitivity can be probed by running multiple trials with different seeds and reporting variance, which is addressable in a rebuttal with additional experiments and analysis without changing the core method.
Requesting additional experimental results on accuracy under input shifts is a standard empirical addition that can be produced and summarized in a rebuttal/appendix without changing the core method.

Requests for details about supplementary experiments and additional validation can be addressed in a rebuttal by summarizing existing but omitted experiments or adding small-scale/new results and clarifying methodologies.

A broad claim that the overall contribution is insufficient empirically or theoretically typically requires new experiments, analyses, proofs, or expanded scope beyond what can be added in a short rebuttal, thus not fully addressable in rebuttal text alone.

This is a conceptual critique about the claimed principledness of the control magnitude; in a rebuttal authors can clarify the interpretation of weights, provide theoretical grounding or calibration procedures, or add guidance/ablation showing intuitive mapping, so it is addressable by explanation and possibly minor analyses without changing the core method.
Clarifying a definition early in the paper is a straightforward editorial change that can be addressed in a rebuttal and revised manuscript without new experiments or theory.

The reviewer asks for analyses linking results to training data, which authors can at least partially address in rebuttal by reporting available metadata, known training sources, or auxiliary experiments on models with known datasets, even if full provenance is unavailable.

Requesting inclusion of all algorithm baselines in figures (or a clear justification for omissions) can be satisfied in a rebuttal by adding plots/tables to the appendix or explaining why some baselines are impractical or incomparable.

Adding missing citations and running or reporting comparisons to specific recent Reader-Retrieval EL work can be addressed in a rebuttal via acknowledgments, clarifications, and possibly providing preliminary comparison results or commitments for the camera-ready.

The concern asks whether results hold when controlling for backbones, which can be addressed in rebuttal by providing controlled ablations or additional experiments using the same backbones as prior work, or at least reporting new numbers or analyses clarifying the backbone effect.

Reporting variability over multiple seeds and adding standard deviations is a straightforward addition to experiments and documentation that can be provided in a rebuttal or camera-ready without changing the core method.

The concern about missing comparisons to improved baselines (e.g., updated GNN implementations from BUDDY) can be addressed by clarifying what versions were used and, if feasible, adding or committing to add results with those baselines or providing reasons for their exclusion.
Authors can add experiments on additional benchmarks or clarify reasons for benchmark choice and provide supplementary results to support generalizability claims within the rebuttal period.

The concern asks for clarification of the method’s distinct value versus specific baselines, which can be addressed in a rebuttal by providing analysis, ablations, metrics, or qualitative advantages not evident from Tables 2–4.
Presentation issues like misleading figures and inconsistent color-coding are straightforward to correct or clarify in a rebuttal and camera-ready revision without altering the core technical claims.

This is a clarification/scope/recommendation issue about why more data wasn’t advocated and how GEMs factor in, which can be addressed in rebuttal by explaining assumptions, data limitations, and adding analysis or discussion points without changing the core method.

Providing a JAX interface or clarifying feasibility and roadmap is an implementation/detail request that can be answered or partially fixed in rebuttal (e.g., committing to add a JAX wrapper or explaining current constraints and planned support).

The concern asks for a clarification of notation and the semantic meaning of Y versus U, which can be directly addressed in a rebuttal by explaining definitions and aligning notation or promising minor edits.
This is a descriptive summary of the paper’s novelty rather than a criticism; there is no actionable concern to rebut, so nothing needs fixing or conceding.
Grammar and writing-quality issues can be corrected during rebuttal and camera-ready editing without changing the technical content.

Hyperparameter selection procedures can be clarified and, if needed, augmented with additional ablation or a brief grid/random-search description in a rebuttal without changing the core method or new experiments beyond small sweeps.

A critique about lack of novelty is primarily about the paper’s conceptual contribution and cannot be resolved in a short rebuttal beyond arguing positioning or reframing, which typically requires a concession or substantial new work.
Terminology ambiguity about the meaning of “passage” can be clarified in rebuttal and fixed by defining terms and adjusting wording without altering the method or results.

A request for deeper discussion of mechanisms and insights can be addressed in a rebuttal by adding explanatory analysis, ablations, or hypothesized mechanisms without changing the core method or results.
